mnemonics.title: Nyquist Formula
mnemonics.content: N2B: N(i)2B (N-i stands for Number of signal levels, 2B for 2 times Bandwidth)

mnemonics.title: Bit vs Baud
mnemonics.content: B Bit = Bits per second; B Baud = Beats (changes) per second

acronyms.usage: Measures signal quality; higher SNR means better signal clarity
Range of frequencies a channel can transmit; measured in Hz
Line coding scheme where signal doesn't return to zero between bits
Encoding scheme using alternating polarity for 1s
Modulation technique using phase changes to represent data

visualTips.visualization: Think of analog as a smooth rolling hill (continuous) and digital as a staircase (discrete steps)
Picture waves on water - height of the wave from middle to peak is amplitude
Imagine a pendulum swinging - number of complete swings per second is frequency
Think of a highway - wider highway (more bandwidth) allows more cars (data) at once
Like sound getting quieter as you move away from the source
Imagine a traffic light - can change faster with higher bandwidth, carrying more cars (bits)

analogies.analogy: Bandwidth is like the width of a highway - a 4-lane highway can handle more cars (data) than a 1-lane road in the same time.
Like the mercury in a thermometer - it moves continuously and can take any value between points.
Like a light switch - it's either ON (1) or OFF (0), no in-between states.
Like trying to hear someone in a crowded room - the background noise (other conversations) makes it harder to understand the message.
Think of filling a leaky bucket - bandwidth is the bucket size, signal-to-noise ratio is how much water (data) you can add before it overflows (gets corrupted).
Like shouting across a large field - your voice (signal) gets weaker (attenuated) the further it travels.
Like starting a race from different positions on the track - you're running the same race (frequency) but at different starting points (phase).
